summary: Justin Brennan coaches on multifamily deal underwriting through two detailed case studies: Julian's closed 23-unit deal in Michigan ($1.15M final price after $150K price reduction via due diligence), and Jason's Cincinnati 31-unit prospect. Key focus areas include financial due diligence red flags, proper expense modeling, and capital raise execution.
takeaways: Use $600-$800 per unit for repairs & maintenance on pre-1980 buildings; don't accept artificially low numbers from sellers
Request insurance quotes from vendors upfront (quote process takes 2-3 days) rather than relying on seller's rates that may reflect master policies you cannot access
Property taxes and insurance are the two largest expense line items—verify both independently; use AI tools for tax verification
Demand to see non-operational expenses line item on P&L; if seller refuses, it signals hidden R&M costs being misclassified
For 1950-1980 vintage properties, expect 10-12% economic vacancy/credit loss even in 93% occupied scenarios; use historical data to justify
Ask directly for commitment after presenting: 'Are we doing $50K or $100K?' vs. pause; isolate objections into three buckets: timing/capital, trust/comfort, or deal quality
Get insurance quotes early at $400-$600/unit baseline for market; flood insurance can add $5,000-$25,000/year—verify before LOI
Build 10-15 LOIs in pipeline; don't be too selective; broker conversations and seller motivation will determine which land
Extension options critical: Julian used 30-day extension (90 days total vs. standard 60) when capital raise tightened—ensure contracts allow this
Verify rent rolls show actual current rents for occupied units; separate vacant units into line items and assume market rent for underwriting purposes

skill_candidates.change: Establish systematic P&L verification checklist targeting the two largest expense lines (taxes & insurance) and catch revenue manipulation via bad-debt misclassification
skill_candidates.evidence: Julian discovered seller was counting uncollected bad debt as income on the proforma; Justin demonstrated that requesting non-operational expense line forces transparency; audience shown $600-800/unit R&M benchmark for vintage buildings

skill_candidates.change: Use vintage-based expense modeling rather than accepting seller financials; back into realistic expense ratio (40-50% for 1980+ properties) when detail is unavailable
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin showed how Granada property with artificially low 29% expense ratio should operate at 50-51%; demonstrated using industry benchmarks (1980 or older = 40-50% ratio) to override seller assumptions

skill_candidates.change: Develop vendor network for independent validation (insurance brokers, inspectors, lenders) and call brokers systematically with underwritten offer rather than waiting for perfect information
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin called three brokers live during session, identified Granada property with 27-year hold and flood zone issue; secured direct contact (Jerry Brewer) for future deal flow; recommended $21-22M offer on Cincinnati property despite gaps

skill_candidates.change: Execute direct closing logistics (utility transfers, property manager coordination, insurance procurement) in real-time rather than delegating entirely to lawyers; identify tasks that don't require legal review
skill_candidates.evidence: Julian admitted mistake of not transferring utilities to his name before close—tenants lost electricity; emphasized lawyer costs ($2K+) on routine items that could be handled direct; legal team member recommended calling seller directly on factual questions

title: Weekly Coaching_05/07/26 | Justin Brennan
